chapters.title: Social shaming: being gay is still challenging sometimes
chapters.text:
Evidently, the gay community’s struggle for rights has started to pay off and changed lives for the better. It was a difficult and tiring path from the illegality of homosexuality, the threat of imprisonment, and the need to hide one's orientation even from the closest people. Today it seems easier for gay people to live freely and fully, and many succeed. They build happy relationships and find their place in the world. However, assuming their lives lack difficulties and challenges would be a mistake.

Regardless of our orientation, race, or origin, we all deserve support in striving to be ourselves.

Studies show that many LGBTQ people today struggle with alcohol, drugs, sex addiction, depression, eating disorders, and other psychological problems. Where are the roots of this suffering, and why are some people with non-traditional orientations forced to fight for the right to be themselves?

Love is fundamentally pivotal to the survival of all human beings. ~ Matthew Todd

Throughout this summary, you will get a glimpse into the peculiarities of growing up as a gay person, the self-awareness involved, and the complex and sometimes overwhelming feelings they face. You will learn how parents and society contribute to the emergence of destructive shame in some LGBTQ people and how they cope with it. Matthew Todd shows how painful the experiences of gay people can be and the possible consequences. But despite all the difficulties and internal conflict that some LGBT people have, there is hope for a better and happier life. The journey to self-discovery is long and winding, but it can change everything.

chapters.title: It can be traumatic growing up as a gay person
chapters.text:
The first difficulty gay people may face in childhood is realizing their otherness. Often this happens in the pre-sexual stage when their behavior and interests do not correspond to traditional ideas about boys and girls.

In childhood, our self-perception entirely depends on our parents. From birth, our survival instincts ensure we are closely connected with them. There are two ways in which parents shape children's self-awareness:

1. Mirroring: When the baby smiles, the parents repeat their emotions and hug and kiss them back. This exchange makes them both happy and promotes intense contact.
2. Shame: This is when parents forbid children from doing certain things, such as touching a hot iron or saying bad words. In this way, they teach them social norms. It's a healthy process where a child learns that they're not perfect, but that's okay.

The families and friends of gay people often recognize their homosexuality before they do.

However, there is another type of shame — toxic, which can be highly damaging. Parents sometimes unconsciously convey to the child that it is not their actions that are wrong, but themselves. There are many reasons parents can toxically shame their children. It can happen due to adults' psychological and mental state, financial problems, and exhaustion. There is also the opinion that toxic shame is passed down from generation to generation. Any child can go through this, regardless of their orientation, but gay people may experience it when their parents become aware of their otherness. Adults don't have to voice it because the child will understand based on emotional and non-verbal signals. Shaming begins from this moment, and the “I'm bad” attitude is deeply rooted.

A child often relies heavily on their parents to the extent that they may find it hard to question their parents' viewpoints. Suppose they are not fond of playing football or displaying aggressive behavior. In that case, they may perceive it as a personal issue they cannot resolve rather than considering it a matter of personal preference.

Over time, gay people become aware of their sexuality and face that their choices here are not what others expect. Shame becomes part of their personality and does not disappear later. And while there is nothing wrong with being a homosexual, LGBT people feel shame because society has rigid ideas about what is “normal.” It is only the beginning of their struggle for the right to be themselves.

chapters.title: Why gay people sometimes feel unsafe
chapters.text:
Shame is not the only problem LGBTQ people may face because it triggers other processes and can lead to deep traumatization. When people feel like they are wrong, they become highly vulnerable, reducing their self-esteem. Shame may subconsciously make a child feel like they are the cause of all misfortune or a threat. It's no wonder that anxiety can become a gay person’s companion. This constant internal discomfort and restlessness can lead to negative thoughts and hypervigilance. Matthew Todd recalls not being able to relax or sleep due to anxiety. He felt different from others and worried he would be “exposed.”

Anxiety is not a natural human condition. It is appropriate only when there is a real threat to life. Our “fight or flight” response is normal when we see a car coming at us or a predator trying to attack. In this mode, we feel a surge of strength and energy we need to survive. The problem is that we can't always distinguish between real and imagined danger. Perceiving themselves as a threat due to constant shaming, gay people can fall into a vicious circle of anxiety, from which it is difficult to break out without help and support.

The Gay Pride movement makes us think coming out is just about being proud of oneself but it's also about overcoming the intense fear of being different.

In addition to internal personal reasons contributing to constant restlessness, anxiety has external factors. Despite progress in society's acceptance of gay people, homophobic attitudes still exist and can pose a real threat to the LGBTQ community. There are still cases of homophobic bullying in schools, and some parents cannot come to terms with their child's sexual orientation. It applies not only to physical violence but also emotional, which can lead to terrible consequences. Bullying is a common problem, but for gay individuals, it's even worse — they may feel they can't defend themselves because of the fear of exposure.

Realizing non-traditional orientation is a stressful process that requires special attention and support. Of course, many gay people have understanding and accepting parents, but there are still those who have to survive and find ways to cope with emotional tension. Unfortunately, these methods aren't always adaptive and can cause more problems.

chapters.title: Breaking the gay stereotype
chapters.text:
The term 'gay' means fun, carefree, and bright — and that's how media and films usually portray gay people — those who enjoy life and party hard. Although there are many joy-sparkling representatives of the gay community, this view overlooks the other side. For some LGBTQ people, parties, clubs, and alcohol are ways to escape from emotional problems and reduce inner anxiety, at least for a while. The desire to avoid complicated feelings often leads to various kinds of addictions.

Psychological trauma experienced in childhood increases the risk of addictive behavior in adulthood.

When facing something unbearable, the human psyche reacts in one of three ways:

1. Splitting means the person rejects or ignores a particular event or experience. For example, a gay person may deny their sexual feelings for another man, claiming it's temporary.
2. Codependency involves excessive attachment to other people and difficulties in building healthy relationships. People who use this defensive method have low self-esteem and lack self-confidence.
3. Counterdependency is the reverse of the previous point and means pushing other people away and denying the need for close contact. Counterdependent people often give the impression of being happy and independent when, in reality, they are in pain.

Mental trauma and emotional vulnerability can lead to alcohol and drug addiction. When people do not have psychological problems and feel good most of the time, alcohol may slightly increase their happiness. At the same time, traumatized or anxious people might experience the dramatic effects of alcohol or drug use and feel extreme relief. It’s no wonder it can be difficult for them to relinquish their desire to feel this ease again. This also partly explains the number of addictions among gay people.

By hiding their pain behind entertainment and fun, some gay individuals put themselves at significant risk. Matthew Todd notes that their closest people often do not even know about the problems they sometimes go through. Their behavior can sometimes be so destructive that it leads to suicide. A gay person’s life can be unbearable if they ignore psychological trauma, but there's always a way out.

Did you know? According to Statista, people in Great Britain are more likely to support a family member or friend who comes out as gay, lesbian, or bisexual. Italy and Germany seem to be the least supportive.

chapters.title: Alcohol and drugs don't make it all
chapters.text:
We often think that only psychoactive substances can be addictive, but in reality, it can be anything that increases dopamine. In general, there are two types of addiction:

1. Substances — alcohol, drugs, food, caffeine, or medicines.
2. Processes — shopping, sex, relationships, falling in love, work, or masturbation.

Anything that gives a person a sense of relief and control can be addictive.

A challenging childhood and shame carried into adulthood can lead to destructive behavior:

• Eating disorders. There can be too much food (bulimia) or not enough (anorexia), which indicates internal struggles. Even excessive exercise and a focus on the body can be a sign of an eating disorder.
• Self-harm. Self-inflicted physical harm, like minor cuts, can be a way to reduce internal tension and emotional pain.
• Escapism. This process means avoiding and ignoring reality by immersing in movies, books, or dreams. It also includes fanatical admiration for singers, actors, or fictional characters who have gone through similar difficulties of self-identification.
• Sex addiction. The process and preparation for sex — finding a partner and a place — can be obsessive. While there is nothing wrong with pleasure, sexual addiction often ignores the emotional intimacy a person may seek.
• Shopping too much. Buying new things also releases dopamine and provides desired satisfaction, but it is only a form of escapism from a real inner need.
• The need for confirmation. This is a constant search for praise, the desire to be first, and to get many likes and positive comments on social media. The need for endless validation can be due to insecurity and self-loathing.

Although these behavior patterns help the traumatized person find inner balance and peace for a while, they still do not solve the real problem. Of course, they help them to survive and deal with the pain, but obsessing only worsens the situation. Addiction is dangerous because it can harm health and relationships with loved ones and deepen psychological trauma. The good news is that noticing and recognizing an addiction, or any repetitive behavior, can get a person closer to the help they need.

The gay appeal of certain artists isn't because we know they had difficult lives. It's because we sense in them the vulnerability and strength that we have too. ~ Matthew Todd

chapters.title: There is always a way out
chapters.text:
Recognizing addiction is the first step to asking for serious help. It requires courage, strength, and the support of friends and relatives. Matthew Todd notes that some gay men he knew felt their behavior changed when they reached a critical point. They realized they didn't want to wake up in a stranger's apartment or be on the verge of death from drug or alcohol use.

Life can't be changed overnight, but there are tangible steps people can take.

Staying strong and remembering that things don't have to be this way is crucial. Here are some options that help to break out of the cycle of addiction:

• Medical assistance. Eating disorders, depression, drugs, and alcohol can seriously harm a person's health, so seeking professional help and undergoing a complete medical examination is essential. This step is also significant for those struggling with sexual addiction, as the problem of HIV and AIDS is still severe and widespread.
• Rehabilitation. This method of fighting addiction is a strong option because it involves a comprehensive approach. It usually takes place in a quiet, remote location and includes various group and art therapy activities. The only disadvantage of this method is its high cost.
• Antidepressants. Medications may be appropriate for combating anxiety and depressive disorders but usually require a doctor's consultation. A common problem with this method is that it dulls not only stress and fear, but also positive feelings.
• Psychotherapy. There are several options: a free therapist with a waiting list or a private one. In the second case, there is an opportunity to choose and look for a professional specializing in addiction. It is also essential to find a comfortable therapeutic method. Many professionals recommend a CBT approach focusing on developing new behavior patterns, although working with trauma often requires more long-term and in-depth therapy.
• Support groups. There are free or low-cost group support options for LGBTQ people that can be extremely helpful.

Struggling with addiction is not the only reason to seek help. Emotional suffering, loneliness, doubts, and fears are enough to find a therapist or a support group. Accepting one's otherness due to sexual orientation or fighting social shame is complicated and deserves exceptional attention.

chapters.title: There’s no need to go through the changes alone
chapters.text:
Matthew Todd has come a long way from being ashamed of his sexuality to being his true self. After trying different methods and evaluating their benefits, he emphasizes the positives of the Twelve Steps program. He notes that it was there that he managed to find the safe space and support that gay people need on the way to change. This program has received worldwide recognition as one of the best methods for fighting addictions.

Twelve Steps is a series of informal meetings between people seeking to break free from addiction, and they resemble group therapy. There is no leader or coach; the participants independently organize the rehabilitation process. As Todd points out, one of the advantages of this method is that there is no coercion because the participants share their stories and feelings only if they want to. In addition, they have the right to be late for meetings, leave early, or not go at all; the process builds on mutual trust and respect for the needs of the participants. Here are some advantages of this method:

• Availability. There is no fixed price to pay. The program works on a charitable basis, and each participant makes a financial contribution at their discretion. In addition, these groups are available in almost every city and have no waiting lists, so you can join anytime.
• Lack of obligations. Participants only have a responsibility to themselves. There is no risk of losing money due to absence, as in private therapy. However, regular meetings will bring the most significant benefit.
• Anonymity. One of the program's rules is to preserve the confidentiality of other participants, which aims to guarantee a safe space and create mutual trust.

Even though there are special groups for LGBTQ people, mainstream meetings offer a safe space regardless of orientation.

The greatest strength of the Twelve Step program is the network of support participants provide for each other. Here they can find a person who can help at a critical moment. Matthew Todd believes this program benefits gay individuals because it encourages sharing the society-inflicted shame of their sexuality, reduces anxiety, and increases self-confidence. Joining the Twelve Step program is a personal decision, but it's important to remember that the fight against addiction and the path to self-acceptance requires support.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Being gay isn't a choice; it's something a person is born with. It's as unique as height or eye color, but society sometimes seems rigid about it. Being aware of the painful experiences gay people often go through since childhood is the first step to changing and creating safe conditions for everyone to thrive. Society must be mindful of their attitudes towards gay people and work hard to create a safe and inclusive environment. People must also look for ways to educate themselves and others about acceptance and respect. Finally, they must ensure everyone can live with dignity, regardless of their sexual orientation.

For society to become better and more tolerant, a few measures should be implemented to help the gay community:

• Combating HIV and AIDS with a new effective program
• Supporting gay men with HIV
• Adaptation programs for young gay people in schools

There are also some questions that the gay community should review:

• Strengthening mutual support and assistance
• Fostering respect between different age groups
• Promoting diversity and eradicating racism within the community

Positive changes can only happen if people make joint efforts, regardless of sexual orientation. Of course, it will be a long road of transformation. However, it guarantees that even more gay people can live happily and finally overcome the unjustified sense of shame that still accompanies some today.

Try this


1. See your doctor for a general health check if you have problems with eating behavior or addictions.
2. Use meditation or mantras if you experience anxiety or sleep disturbances.
3. Pay attention to your inner state and body sensations to identify your needs. This awareness will allow you to reduce tension and gain pleasure.

Read it till the end?
